content:
2 min read
Just now

--

For those following our ongoing investigations and coverage of proposed data centers development at the Muskingum River Industrial Park in Waterford Township, we now have official confirmation of both the existence of the original ground lease and purchase option — starting with BIT56 LLC and later assigned to Muskingum Land Holdco LLC, including all amendments— and its termination.
In response to our questions, Jesse Roush, CEO and President of the Southeastern Ohio Port Authority, provided the records and and in depth answers to a list of the communities questions, from which we have benefited greatly, and will also report on, fully.
Thank you, Jesse, for engaging openly and directly, and for sharing the needed documents.
What the Records Show
On June 22, 2026, Washington County recorded the Termination and Release of Memorandum of Option Agreement and Memorandum of Ground Lease Agreement.
Muskingum Land Holdco LLC has terminated the Ground Lease and Option Agreement, and those agreements are no longer in effect.
The Southeastern Ohio Port Authority continues to hold its fee title interest in the property.
The affected parcels (A, B, and C) are river-adjacent land in Waterford Township within the Muskingum River Industrial Park area.
Important Context on Separate Agreements
Jesse emphasized that the former BIT56/Muskingum Land Holdco Ground Lease and Option Agreement, the current HashWorks Option Agreement, and surrounding private land acquisitions are separate agreements involving different entities and different property interests. We note that point, and plan to dive into understanding exactly that.
We will continue examining public records for any connections, overlaps, or distinctions between these companies and projects in the area.
Let’s all continue following the facts and public records, as new developments arise.
Accurate information from source documents helps the community stay informed. MASH Focus remains committed to transparent, independent reporting on these issues affecting Waterford, Morgan County, and the Mid-Ohio Valley.
Tips, records, or additional questions for officials and those involved are always welcome.
— Holly Kellums, MASH Focus — Publication and Network
